summary: Spolsky enthusiastically endorses Six Degrees, a personal knowledge management tool built by former QuarkXPress designers who left after being mistreated by cost-cutting management. He draws a parallel between the crowded knowledge management space and the early search engine era, arguing that Six Degrees has cracked the formula the way Google cracked search. The post also serves as a cautionary tale about Quark's self-destructive decision to replace experienced developers with cheaper offshore labor.
key_points: Software companies' real assets are their people, and replacing experienced developers with cheaper alternatives destroys value, as Quark demonstrated
The former QuarkXPress designers started their own company after being treated as interchangeable, and were acquired by a Vancouver company that gave them autonomy
Six Degrees represents a breakthrough in personal knowledge management, a field Spolsky sees as important but not yet buzzy
Most companies claiming to solve knowledge management will fail, just as most early search engines did before Google found the right formula
money_quotes: When software companies say that 'their capital assets go home every night,' they are serious. Dead serious.

When Quark goes down in flames because the bean-counters pushed out the experienced developers and hired new, cheaper ones 10 time zones away, there will be a great case study in Harvard Business Review to prove it.
When the key designers of QuarkXPress 5.0 got sick of being treated like interchangable code slaves, they started their own shop.
Six Degrees is gonna be Google for the desktop.
Everywhere I look I see people blabbing about how they're going to crack the knowledge management nut.
Only Larry and Sergey figured out the formula to make search actually work.
key_insight: Treating skilled developers as interchangeable commodities drives away the very talent that creates breakthroughs, often straight into the arms of competitors or startups.
